chunks.text: In this world, there are invisible borders of many kinds.
Some are clear, like national borders.
Others are invisible walls within society.
One of these is the " cycle of poverty.
" A child born into a poor family has limited educational opportunities
cannot get a good job, and, even as an adult, cannot escape from poverty.
This is passed on parallel to the next generation.
To break this cycle requires the effort of society as a whole.
Education, medical care, housing, job training.
By one individual's strength alone, it is difficult to rid oneself from poverty.
Bangladesh's Grameen Bank created a new scheme against poverty.
Microfinance — small loans to low - income people.
Money is lent, even without collateral.
The repayment rate is surprisingly high.
It advertised the fact that poor people are not lazy.
Given only the chance, they succeed through their own power.
The founder Muhammad Yunus received the Nobel Peace Prize in 2006.
" To accuse someone of poverty is a matter of human rights
not of economics," he said.
Meanwhile, in developed countries too, a " new poverty " has been created.
The " working poor," who can only manage a minimum life despite long hours.
A " divide " born in education from the income of parents.
Poverty not only torments those who absorb it
but also blocks the development of society as a whole.
A society in which every person can make use of their talent is a sustainable society.
This is not a distant story.
Beside us, even now, someone is fighting against poverty.
What we can do — for each one of us to think about it — is the first step.
